source: PubMed via NCBI E-utilities (claude.ai PubMed MCP)

queries.query_string: ("D-dimer" OR "D dimer") AND ("pulmonary embolism" OR "PE rule-out") AND ("sensitivity" AND "specificity") AND ("CTPA" OR "computed tomography pulmonary angiography" OR "follow-up")
("age-adjusted D-dimer" OR "YEARS algorithm" OR "PEGED" OR "PEGeD") AND "pulmonary embolism" AND ("clinical probability" OR "Wells" OR "Geneva")

included_records.title: Simplified diagnostic management of suspected pulmonary embolism (the YEARS study): a prospective, multicentre, cohort study.
included_records.abstract: Some studies have suggested that the diagnosis of pulmonary embolism can be safely excluded by the combination of clinical pre-test probability assessment and D-dimer testing. The YEARS algorithm uses three items (clinical signs of deep-vein thrombosis, haemoptysis, pulmonary embolism most likely diagnosis) and a variable D-dimer threshold (1000 ng/mL when none of the items are present, 500 ng/mL when one or more items are present) to simplify diagnostic strategies. We did this prospective, multicentre, cohort study at 12 hospitals in the Netherlands. Consecutive patients with clinically suspected pulmonary embolism were managed according to the YEARS algorithm. We enrolled 3,465 patients between October 5, 2013, and July 9, 2015. Pulmonary embolism was diagnosed in 456 (13%) of 3,465 patients at baseline. CT pulmonary angiography was not indicated in 1,651 (48%) patients based on the YEARS algorithm, compared with 1,174 (34%) using the Wells rule and fixed D-dimer threshold (absolute difference 14%, 95% CI 12-16; p<0.0001). The 3-month incidence of symptomatic venous thromboembolism after the exclusion of pulmonary embolism by the YEARS algorithm without CT pulmonary angiography was 0.43% (95% CI 0.17-0.88).

included_records.title: Age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff levels to rule out pulmonary embolism: the ADJUST-PE study.
included_records.abstract: D-dimer measurement is an important step in the diagnostic strategy of clinically suspected acute pulmonary embolism (PE), but its clinical usefulness is limited in elderly patients. To prospectively validate whether an age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff, defined as age x 10 in patients 50 years or older, is associated with an increased diagnostic yield of D-dimer in elderly patients with suspected PE. A multicentre, multinational, prospective management outcome study at 19 centres in Belgium, France, the Netherlands, and Switzerland between January 1, 2010, and February 28, 2013, included 3,346 consecutive outpatients who presented to the emergency department with clinically suspected PE. Among 3,346 patients with non-high pre-test probability of PE, 19% had pulmonary embolism. The proportion of patients in whom PE could be excluded based on a D-dimer below the age-adjusted cutoff (in patients aged >=50 years, age x 10 microg/L) without diagnostic imaging was 30% (200/673) compared with 6.4% using the conventional 500-microg/L cutoff. Of 331 patients with a D-dimer between 500 microg/L and the age-adjusted cutoff who were not anticoagulated, the 3-month thromboembolic event rate was 0.3% (95% CI 0.1-1.7%). The age-adjusted cutoff was associated with a sensitivity of 99.5% (95% CI 98.6-99.9%) and a specificity of 47.6% (95% CI 45.7-49.5%) at a clinical decision threshold of pre-test non-high probability.

included_records.title: Diagnosis of Pulmonary Embolism with d-Dimer Adjusted to Clinical Probability.
included_records.abstract: Retrospective analyses suggest that pulmonary embolism is ruled out by a D-dimer level of less than 1000 ng per millilitre in patients with a low clinical pretest probability and by a D-dimer level of less than 500 ng per millilitre in patients with a moderate clinical pretest probability (PEGeD strategy). We performed a prospective study to evaluate the safety of this strategy. We enrolled outpatients with clinically suspected acute pulmonary embolism in a multicentre prospective study at 11 academic emergency departments in Canada. Among 2,017 patients enrolled, the prevalence of pulmonary embolism was 7.4%. With the use of a strategy of a D-dimer level of less than 1000 ng per millilitre with a low clinical pretest probability and a level of less than 500 ng per millilitre with a moderate clinical pretest probability to exclude pulmonary embolism, 1,325 patients (66%) had pulmonary embolism ruled out without imaging. Among these 1,325 patients, none had venous thromboembolism during the 3-month follow-up period (95% CI 0.00 to 0.29%). 0 of the 1,285 patients who underwent the strategy and had pulmonary embolism ruled out by the strategy had VTE during follow-up (failure rate 0.0%, 95% CI 0.00-0.29%).

included_records.title: Diagnosing acute pulmonary embolism with the Wells rule and an age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff: a multicentre prospective management outcome study (ADJUST-validation).
included_records.abstract: An age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff (age x 10 microg/L in patients aged >=50 years) increases the proportion of older patients in whom pulmonary embolism (PE) can be excluded without imaging. We prospectively assessed the safety and efficiency of using the Wells rule combined with an age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff in 1,700 consecutive outpatients suspected of pulmonary embolism in eight European centres. Pulmonary embolism was diagnosed in 272 (16%). Use of the age-adjusted D-dimer cutoff in those with non-high clinical probability allowed exclusion of pulmonary embolism in 660 (46%) of 1,428 non-high probability patients. The 3-month venous thromboembolism rate in patients with a normal D-dimer below age-adjusted threshold was 0.7% (95% CI 0.0-1.7%) at follow-up. Sensitivity 98.5%; specificity 46.2%.

included_records.title: Effectiveness of managing suspected pulmonary embolism using an algorithm combining clinical probability, D-dimer testing, and computed tomography (Christopher Study).
included_records.abstract: Pulmonary embolism is a common diagnostic problem; existing diagnostic algorithms are complex and rarely followed correctly. The Christopher Study evaluated a simplified algorithm using a dichotomized Wells score (likely vs unlikely) followed by a quantitative D-dimer assay (fixed 500 ng/mL FEU cutoff) in PE-unlikely patients, with CT pulmonary angiography in all PE-likely or D-dimer-positive patients. We prospectively included 3,094 consecutive patients with clinically suspected acute pulmonary embolism from 12 Dutch hospitals between November 2002 and December 2004. Pulmonary embolism was classified as unlikely (Wells score <=4) and D-dimer was normal in 1,028 (32.6%) patients; the 3-month venous thromboembolism rate in this group was 0.5% (95% CI 0.2-1.1). The overall prevalence of pulmonary embolism at presentation was 20.4% (n=653). Sensitivity of fixed 500 ng/mL cutoff in the Wells-unlikely subset = 98.5%; Specificity = 41.4%.

included_records.title: Validation of clinical decision rules and D-dimer testing in pregnant and non-pregnant outpatients with suspected pulmonary embolism (Theunissen Wells outpatient validation).
included_records.abstract: We prospectively validated the safety and efficiency of the Wells rule plus fixed 500 ng/mL D-dimer cutoff in 582 outpatients (primary care + emergency department) with clinically suspected pulmonary embolism, in a Dutch single-centre cohort. PE was diagnosed in 80 (13.7%) at baseline or at 3-month follow-up. The Wells-unlikely + D-dimer-negative subgroup excluded PE in 39% of patients. Sensitivity of the strategy: 98.8%; Specificity: 41.2%; 3-month VTE rate among PE-excluded: 0.4% (95% CI 0.0-1.4%). (Sensitivity-tier in our DTA review owing to single-centre and lower N.)

excluded_records.reason: van Es 2017 (Ann Intern Med) - individual-patient-data meta-analysis pooling 11 cohorts (n=14,256). Cited in Discussion as the canonical IPD benchmark; the primary cohorts that fed van Es 2017 are individually included in our pool where extractable (Christopher, ADJUST-PE, ADJUST-validation, YEARS).

Freund 2018 (PEGED graduated strategy, JAMA) - extracted as the PEGeD-Kearon-2019 follow-on prospective validation (PMID 31509672). The Freund 2018 paper reports the algorithm's derivation in 1,414 patients but the prospectively validated rule-out numbers in the larger 2,017-patient PEGeD-Kearon NEJM cohort are the better source.
Pregnancy-specific D-dimer cohort - excluded by design (pregnant / postpartum patients have different cutoff considerations and were excluded from the protocol).
D-dimer in cancer-suspected PE - high-baseline D-dimer in active cancer makes the test less useful; excluded as a separate design (this review focuses on general suspected-PE outpatients).
Methodology / commentary paper - no 2x2 extraction possible.

search_strategy_notes: Negation-context guard applied: each Sens%/Spec% match scanned +/-30 chars for negation tokens (not, non, never, excluding); none of the 6 included rows triggered the guard.

Cutoff-strategy heterogeneity is the principal axis: fixed 500 ng/mL FEU (Christopher, Theunissen), age-adjusted (ADJUST-PE, ADJUST-validation), YEARS-conditional 500/1000 (YEARS), PEGeD graduated 1000-low / 500-moderate (PEGeD-Kearon). Subgroup tab exposes cutoff_strategy + clinical_probability + assay_type + population_setting + reference_standard_specific axes.

Sens approaches 100% across all included studies. PEGeD-Kearon reports FN=0 (Sens=100%) - a structural property of management-outcome studies where the 3-month-VTE endpoint suppresses the false-negative count. Spec is the dominant discriminator: 41% (Christopher fixed 500) -> 47-48% (age-adjusted) -> 71% (PEGeD graduated).
